coredata.dc:title: Intramicellar sensor assays: Improving sensing sensitivity of sensor array for thiol biomarkers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Niu, Hongyang
Li, Haichun
Wang, Yi
Hu, Bingtao
Liu, Xue
coredata.dc:description: Array-based analysis allows for precise disease diagnosis by simultaneously detecting multiple biomarkers. However, most array sensing platforms rely on non-covalent interactions between sensors and analytes, which limits their sensitivity. This study enhances the sensitivity of array analysis for thiol biomarkers by incorporating polyion complex micelles into the sensor array design. Polyion complex micelles are formed through the self-assembly of carbon dots (CDs) and anionic diblock copolymers. The sensing detection process can occur inside the core of the micelle, the confined space and ionic environment within the core reduce the interaction distance between CDs, metal ions, and analytes, thereby significantly improving detection sensitivity while minimizing metal ions consumption. The developed array system can accurately identify multiple thiols across a broad concentration range (0.5–1000 μM) with 100% accuracy. Additionally, support vector machine (SVM) analysis reveals a detection limit of approximately 0.1 μM for individual thiol systems. Compared to non-micellar approaches, this micelle-based array reduces the detection limit for thiol biomarkers by 20-fold and decreases Ag+ usage by 40-fold. The system is suitable for both qualitative and quantitative analysis of thiol biomarkers, offering a powerful tool for the accurate diagnosis of thiol-related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sensor array
Polyionic complex micelles
Thiols
Biomarkers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of H-CDs and polyionic complex (PIC) micelles
Qualitative analysis of thiols in serum
Quantitative analysis of thiols in serum
Detection of thiols in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the H-CDs (Inset: size distribution of the H-CDs). (b) TEM image of the mPH-NP-4 (Inset: size distribution of the mPH-NP-4). (c) PL spectra of the mPA-NP-1 (excitation wavelength: 390 nm), mPA-NP-2 (excitation wavelength: 430 nm), mPA-NP-3 (excitation wavelength: 470 nm) and mPA-NP-4 (excitation wavelength: 505 nm). (d) Zeta potential of the H-CDs and the mPH-NPs.
(a) TEM images of the mPH-NPs, mPH-NPs@Ag+, and mPH-NPs@Ag+ after adding GSH. (b) SEM and elemental mapping images of the mPH-NPs, mPH-NPs@Ag+, and mPH-NPs@Ag+ after adding GSH.

(a) 2D PCA plot for response patterns of seven thiols across varying concentrations (data points in identical hues signify equivalent concentration levels). (b) Mean Euclidean distance (ED) metrics of seven thiols across varying concentrations. (c) 2D PCA plot for response patterns of seven thiols (0.5 μM). (d) Heat map of the sensor array against seven thiols from Fig. 3c.
(a) Identification of unknown thiols utilizing the sensor array. (b) 2D PCA graph illustrating response patterns to thiols (1 μM) and various interfering substances (100 μM).

(a, b, c) 2D LDA plots depicting the analysis of GSH (a), Hcy (b), and Cys (c) at different concentrations. (d, e, f) Linear calibration models for the quantitative determination of GSH (d), Hcy (e), and Cys (f) (PC: prediction concentration; PER: prediction error rate). (g, h, i) Quantitative analysis of GSH (g), Hcy (h), and Cys (i) for the single-thiol systems using the SVM.

(a) 2D LDA plots of the sensor array toward various cells with different concentrations of GSH. (b) Linear calibration model for quantitative analysis of GSH in cell (PC: prediction concentration). (c) Discriminant Factor 1 plot. (d) ROC curve demonstrating the sensor array's capability in differentiating cancer from healthy conditions. (e) 2D LDA plots of the sensor array toward clinical serum samples with various concentrations of Hcy. (f) Linear calibration model for quantitative analysis of clinical serum samples with various concentrations of Hcy. (PC: prediction concentration). (g) Plot of the discriminant factor 1 of serums from patients with cardiovascular disease and healthy volunteers. (h) ROC curve demonstrating the sensor array's capability in differentiating cardiovascular disease versus from healthy volunteers. Data are presented as mean ± SD (n = 5). P values were calculated using a two-sided t-test (∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗∗P < 0.0001).
Schematic illustration of the synthetic process and the array sensing mechanism of the mPH-NPs.

head.ce:title: Intramicellar sensor assays: Improving sensing sensitivity of sensor array for thiol biomarkers

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Array-based analysis allows for precise disease diagnosis by simultaneously detecting multiple biomarkers. However, most array sensing platforms rely on non-covalent interactions between sensors and analytes, which limits their sensitivity. This study enhances the sensitivity of array analysis for thiol biomarkers by incorporating polyion complex micelles into the sensor array design. Polyion complex micelles are formed through the self-assembly of carbon dots (CDs) and anionic diblock copolymers. The sensing detection process can occur inside the core of the micelle, the confined space and ionic environment within the core reduce the interaction distance between CDs, metal ions, and analytes, thereby significantly improving detection sensitivity while minimizing metal ions consumption. The developed array system can accurately identify multiple thiols across a broad concentration range (0.5–1000 μM) with 100% accuracy. Additionally, support vector machine (SVM) analysis reveals a detection limit of approximately 0.1 μM for individual thiol systems. Compared to non-micellar approaches, this micelle-based array reduces the detection limit for thiol biomarkers by 20-fold and decreases Ag+ usage by 40-fold. The system is suitable for both qualitative and quantitative analysis of thiol biomarkers, offering a powerful tool for the accurate diagnosis of thiol-related diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The design inspiration for the sensor array comes from the olfactory and gustatory systems of mammals, featuring flexibility in the combination of sensing units, diverse signal output types, and the advantage of efficiently identifying various similar analytes, holding significant application value in the precise disease diagnosis (You et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2023). Most sensor array analyses are based on non-covalent interactions between the sensing units and the analytes (Li et al., 2019b). This weak interaction mode allows the sensing units to interact with various analytes, which is an essential foundation for ensuring the signal abundance in the sensor array analysis system (Broza et al., 2018). However, this weak interaction limits the sensitivity and detection limit of the sensor array analysis method, further affecting the accuracy of the sensor array analysis method in practical applications (Li et al., 2019a; Yuan et al., 2019). Therefore, how to improve the sensitivity of the sensor array system and reduce the detection limit is of great significance for the future development and application of the sensor array system.

Polyelectrolytes, as important polymeric materials, play a crucial role in the construction of sensor arrays due to their unique physical and chemical properties (Liu et al., 2021). By adjusting the molecular weight, cross-linking degree, and functional groups of polyelectrolytes, the design and functionality of sensor arrays are significantly enhanced (Duan et al., 2015). Additionally, polyelectrolytes also serve as indicators and receptors in the construction of sensor arrays (Tomita, 2022). For example, Tomita et al. construct sensing units by electrostatically assembling cationic polyelectrolytes with anionic enzymes, and the addition of protein analytes triggers competitive interactions between the anionic enzyme, cationic polyelectrolyte, and protein analytes. These interactions lead to the reversible inhibition of enzyme activity, enabling the detection and identification of target proteins (Tomita et al., 2016). With their unique ion-exchange capabilities, excellent stability, and adjustable chemical structures, polyelectrolytes demonstrate wide application potential in sensor analysis. In constructing sensor arrays, the application of polyelectrolytes effectively enhances the sensitivity and specificity of the sensors (Li et al., 2023). Despite their multiple advantages, research on the application of polyelectrolytes in sensor arrays, particularly involving the self-assembly of polyelectrolytes with oppositely charged components to form polyionic complex (PIC) micelles, is quite rare. PIC micelles possess a hydration layer that allows stable binding between the fluorescent material and fluorescence modulator without affecting their functions, thus enhancing the stability and lifespan of the sensor. Moreover, they reduce environmental interferences on the sensing system, improving the sensor's stability and anti-interference capability. The restricted space and ionic environment within these PIC micelles can shorten the interaction distance between the CDs, metal ions, and analyte, potentially increasing the sensitivity and lowering the detection limit of sensor array analysis.

Thiol biomarkers regulate numerous physiological functions, and their abnormal levels directly correlate with multiple diseases (Feng et al., 2020). For instance, elevated glutathione (GSH) levels in human blood, beyond the normal range of 2–20 μM, are associated with diseases like Alzheimer's, acquired immune deficiency syndrome, and cancer (Lei et al., 2016). Similarly, increased homocysteine (Hcy) levels, exceeding the normal range of 5–15 μM, significantly heighten the risk of arteriosclerosis, coronary heart disease, and thromboembolism. Blood Hcy concentrations above 15 μM are indicative of hyperhomocysteinemia (Zhu et al., 2022). In our previous work, we developed a sensor array using carbon dots (CDs) and Ag+ for thiol biomarker detection (Wu et al., 2017). However, the detection limit of 10 μM, influenced by the binding effect between metal ions and fluorescent CDs, failed to meet clinical standards for several related diseases. Thus, an efficient, highly sensitive integrated thiol analysis system is vital for non-invasive diagnosis and monitoring of thiol-related diseases. Inspired by the electrostatic interaction between cell membrane surface receptors and exogenous substances, we self-assembled cationic H-CDs with anionic diblock copolymers of polyethylene glycol-b-polyaspartic acid (PEG-b-PAsp) with varying PAsp molecular weights into four types of polyionic complex micelles (mPH-NPs) with different CDs loadings. These mPH-NPs were combined with an equal amount of Ag+ (as a fluorescence modulator) to achieve a dynamic quenching effect, formed differential sensing units for a thiol biomarker detection sensor array (Scheme 1 ). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) verified that the sensing detection primarily occurred within the micelle core, enhancing reaction stability. Remarkably, this integrated sensing system precisely identified and quantified both single-component and multi-component mixtures of GSH, Hcy, and Cys in serum samples. Furthermore, the sensor array effectively distinguishes between normal and cancer cells based on different GSH levels, and identifies cardiovascular disease patients by detecting variations in Hcy content in serum samples, demonstrating its potential in disease diagnosis.

Poly(ethylene glycol methyl ether) (PEG113-OH, Mn = 5000 Da, Mw/Mn = 1.05, Purity ≥99%), Toluenesulfonyl chloride (Purity ≥97%), and Potassium 1,3-dioxoisoindolin-2-ide (Purity ≥97%) are purchased from Aladdin and used after dried under vacuum. Triphosgene (Purity ≥99%) is provided by Shanghai Ruiteng Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine (Purity ≥99%) and hydrazine monohydrate (Purity ≥98%) are ordered from YongDa Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). L-aspartate acid (Purity ≥99%), L-histidine acid (Purity ≥99%), and urea (Purity ≥99%) are obtained from InnoChem Science&Technology Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Mercaptoacetic acid (MA-SH, Purity ≥97%), 2-mercaptoethanol (ME-SH, Purity ≥97%), mercaptosuccinic acid (MS-SH, Purity ≥99%), cysteamine hydrochloride (CH-SH, Purity ≥99%), glutathione (GSH, Purity ≥97%), cysteine (Cys, Purity ≥97%), and homocysteine (Hcy, Purity ≥99%) are ordered from Titan Science&Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaNO3, Mg(NO3)2, Ca(NO3)2, KNO3, AgNO3, glucose (Purity ≥98%), ascorbic acid (Purity ≥99%), dopamine (Purity ≥98%), uric acid (Purity ≥99%), glycine (Purity ≥99%), tryptophan (Purity ≥95%), proline (Purity ≥95%), bovine serum albumin (Purity ≥98%), and thrombin (Purity ≥95%) are purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS, Purity: 100%) is purchased from Li&Li Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Penicillin-Streptomycin (P/S) Solution is purchased from Li Rui Biological Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All cell culture media are purchased from Corning Co., Ltd. (Corning, USA). Human homocysteine ELISA kit was obtained from Meiao biological technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Total glutathione assay kit was obtained from Beyotime Biotechnology Ltd., Co. (Shanghai, China). Dialysis membrane is ordered from Baoman Biological Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals are analytical reagents and used directly without further purification. All experimental reagents are analytical reagents. Ultrapure water is used throughout all experiments. All the other reagents are purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). All the solvents are of analytical grade and used as received.

TEM image, SEM image, and element mapping are obtained on a JEM-2100F microscope (JEOL). Photoluminescence measurements are executed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-5301PC, SHIMADZU, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra are recorded on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU-1900, Purkinje General, China). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) are recorded using a K-alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, UK). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra are recorded on a PerkinElmer FTIR spectrometer with KBr pellets. 1H-NMR spectrums are obtained by a Bruker DMX 300 MHz spectrometer using tetramethylsilane as the internal standard and D2O, DMSO-d
6 as the solvent. Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) is used to analyze the molecular weights and molecular weight distributions (Mw/Mn) of the polymers. GPC of the copolymer is measured at room temperature with a Waters 1515 chromatograph equipped with a Waters 2414 refractive index detector. Tetrahydrofuran is used as eluent with a flow rate of 0.6 mL/min and narrowly distributed polyethylene glycol is used as standard. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements are performed on a laser light scattering spectrometer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern, UK) equipped with a laser source of 560 nm. Microwave reactions are conducted using a domestic microwave oven (Midea Microwave 20 L, Digital, M1-L202B, Foshan, China) at a power of 750 W.

Synthesis procedures for block copolymers of PEG113-b-PAsp used in this study are shown in Fig. S1. The β-benzyl-L-aspartate N-carboxy anhydride (BLA-NCA) is synthesized employing the Fuchs-Farthing method (Dou et al., 2014). Triphosgene and BLA are combined in anhydrous 1,4-dioxane under a nitrogen atmosphere, maintained at 60 °C until the mixture clarifies. Subsequently, anhydrous n-hexane is gradually introduced into this mixture. The resulting solution undergoes recrystallization at −20 °C overnight. The BLA-NCA crystals are then dissolved in ethyl acetate and precipitated using n-hexane. The final step involves drying the resultant white, needle-like BLA-NCA crystals under vacuum conditions.

Poly(ethylene glycol)-b-poly(β-benzyl-L-aspartate) (PEG-b-PBLA) block copolymers, varying in PBLA chain lengths, are synthesized through ring-opening polymerization (ROP) of BLA-NCA, using PEG113-NH2 as an initiator. Initially, BLA-NCA is thoroughly dissolved in dry CH2Cl2, to which the initiator, already dissolved in CH2Cl2, is added. This mixture is then stirred for 72 h at 35 °C under a dry nitrogen atmosphere. The resultant crude products are precipitated in a 10-fold excess of cold diethyl ether and subsequently isolated by filtration. Post two washes with diethyl ether, these products are vacuum-dried. The poly(ethylene glycol)-b-poly(aspartic acid) (PEG-b-PAsp) block copolymers, featuring varied PAsp chain lengths, are synthesized by deprotecting PEG-b-PBLA in a NaOH solution. After 10 h of stirring at room temperature, the solution is neutralized using HCl and concentrated via vacuum evaporation. Dialysis of this concentrated solution against water, using a dialysis bag with an appropriate molecular cut-off, leads to the formation of PEG-b-PAsp of differing molecular weights, which are then obtained through lyophilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of H-CDs and polyionic complex (PIC) micelles
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (H-CDs) are synthesized using a microwave heating method. The specific steps are as follows: Firstly, 5 mmol of L-histidine acid and 10 mmol of urea are dissolved in 5 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture is then heated in a domestic microwave oven at 700 W for 4 min. After cooling the reaction product to room temperature, centrifugation is used to remove impurities. The solution is dialyzed for 48 h using a dialysis membrane in ultrapure water to remove excess precursors and unreacted reactants. Finally, the dialysate is collected and freeze-dried using a vacuum freeze dryer to obtain the H-CDs powder, which is stored at 4 °C for future use.

Polyionic complex (PIC) micelles are synthesized from H-CDs and block copolymers through the following steps: PIC micelles are formed by the electrostatic interaction of H-CDs with the block copolymer. Briefly, 800 mg of H-CDs is dissolved in 40 mL of ultrapure water in a beaker and stirred. Subsequently, a solution of the block copolymer (PEG-b-PAsp) (10 mg/mL, 5 mL) is added dropwise into the H-CDs solution, followed by a reaction for 8 h. The reaction solution is then dialyzed against water in a dialysis bag with an appropriate molecular cutoff for 48 h, with the water changed every 12 h. Finally, the mPH-NPs are obtained after freeze-drying and are maintained at 4 °C for further use. Different loading capacities of PIC micelle solutions can be achieved by using PEG-b-PAsp with varying molecular weights.

The absorbance of all mPH-NPs@Ag+ solutions at their respective optimal excitation wavelengths is adjusted to 0.1, and the fluorescence spectra of each mPH-NP@Ag+ solution at the optimal excitation wavelength are detected simultaneously. Subsequently, different concentrations of mercaptoacetic acid (MA-SH), 2-mercaptoethanol (ME-SH), mercaptosuccinic acid (MS-SH), cysteamine hydrochloride (CH-SH), glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), and homocysteine (Hcy) solutions are prepared and added to 5 mL of mPH-NPs@Ag+ buffer solutions, and the fluorescence spectra of each mixed solution are recorded simultaneously. The fluorescence response spectrum ((I0-I)/I0) is obtained by performing five replicate tests for each detection analyte and sensing unit, and the obtained data are normalized, with I0 indicating the fluorescence intensity in the absence of thiol and I indicating the fluorescence intensity after the addition of thiol. The final sensor matrix data (4 sensors × 7 thiols × 5 replicates) are obtained, and then the data are further evaluated using SPSS v26.0.

Three thiols are randomly selected from a set of seven thiol specimens as unknown samples for examination by the sensor array. Upon adding 10 μL of each unknown sample solution to 5 mL of mPH-NPs@Ag+ buffer solution, fluorescence signals are detected and recorded. The collected fluorescence response signals are normalized to produce the fluorescence response spectrum ((I0-I)/I0), and the predictive data for the unknown samples are computed. Finally, the Mahalanobis distance between the unknown samples and the initial training samples is calculated to ascertain the classification of the unknown samples.

A variety of potential interfering compounds possibly found in human biological samples, including proteins (thrombin and bovine serum albumin), ions (Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, and K+), amino acids (aspartic acid, proline, tryptophan, and glycine), and biomolecules (uric acid, ascorbic acid, dopamine, glucose, and urea) are selected for analysis. The analytical procedure involves successively adding 10 μL of a solution containing thiol and interfering substances at specific concentrations (1 μM for thiol and 100 μM for interfering substances) to 5 mL of mPH-NPs@Ag+ solution. The resultant data are normalized and analyzed using SPSS v26.0.

To remove proteins, human serum samples are first pre-treated by adding acetonitrile (500 μL) to the serum sample (200 μL), and then the mixture is centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min. Various thiol samples are prepared by dissolving distinct thiols in serum. Each thiol test sample is created by adding 10 μL of thiol solution to 5 mL of mPH-NPs@Ag+ solution. The fluorescence spectra of the mPH-NPs@Ag+ solution and the thiol test specimens are captured and normalized. The ensuing normalized luminescence response values ((I0-I)/I0) undergo evaluation via Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) using SPSS v26.0. For identifying binary and ternary mixtures of thiols in serum, solutions of GSH, Hcy, and Cys are prepared in varying molar ratios and added individually to 5 mL of mPH-NPs@Ag+ solution, ensuring a total thiol concentration of 10 μM in each sample. The collected FL response data are then analyzed using LDA in SPSS v26.0.

GSH, Hcy, and Cys solutions are prepared by dissolving the thiols in the same serum environment as mentioned above. For the quantitative assessment of individual thiols, standard specimens at six varied concentrations (1 μM, 20 μM, 60 μM, 150 μM, 200 μM, and 300 μM) are utilized to construct a calibration curve, which is subsequently verified with sample concentrations of 100 μM and 180 μM. After five repeated tests for each concentration, the fluorescence response data are normalized and processed using LDA. Simultaneously, the support vector machine (SVM) model is employed to perform regression analysis on the collected eight sets of fluorescence response data, achieving accurate concentration quantification in the single-thiol system. Then, twelve sets of ternary mixtures with different concentration ratios of GSH, Hcy, and Cys are prepared in the same serum environment as mentioned above, for the quantitative analysis of these thiol mixtures, among which nine groups are designated as the training set for calibration and modeling, while the remaining three groups are used as the test set for cross-validation. The analysis results, after ten repetitions and data normalization, are further processed using the SVM in MATLAB v9.3.

The integrated sensing system differentiated various cell types with different GSH contents, including normal lung tissue cells (BEAS-2B), normal liver cells (L02), lung cancer cells (A549), cervical cancer cells (HeLa), and liver cancer cells (HepG2). The specific operation is as follows: All cells are seeded in 6-well plates at a density of 2 × 105 cells per well and incubated for 24 h in RPMI medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% antibiotics (penicillin/streptomycin). The cells are washed three times with PBS buffer to obtain a cell suspension. Subsequently, the cells are counted and the total number is calculated to ensure the accuracy of the standard analysis with the GSH assay kit and sensor detection experiments. Then, the cells are lysed by ultrasonication (5 s each time at 10 s intervals for a total of 20 times). After centrifuging and lysing the cells, the total glutathione content inside the cells is determined using a total glutathione assay kit. First, the cell lysate is diluted 20 times with protein removal agent S. Then, 150 μL of glutathione assay working solution and 50 μL of NADPH solution are sequentially added to a 96-well plate and mixed thoroughly. A microplate reader is used to measure the absorbance every 5 min to collect data and plot the standard curve. Furthermore, in the sensor detection experiment, the cell lysate is diluted 10 times with culture medium, incubated with mPH-NPs@Ag+ solution for 30 min, and then the fluorescence intensity is measured with a fluorescence spectrophotometer. Subsequently, the fluorescence response data are normalized and analyzed using LDA in SPSS v26.0. To distinguish cardiovascular disease patients from healthy volunteers, a competitive enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) is used to detect the Hcy content in the serum of 13 volunteers collected from the hospital. First, the serum samples are pretreated by adding 500 μL of acetonitrile to 200 μL of serum, followed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 20 min. The Hcy calibrators and test samples are added to a microplate pre-coated with anti-Hcy antibodies (solid-phase antibodies), followed by the addition of HRP-labeled Hcy antigens (enzyme-labeled antigens). After incubation and thorough washing to remove unbound components, an immune complex of solid-phase antibodies and enzyme-labeled antigens forms on the surface of the microplate. Substrates are then added, and the absorbance is measured at a wavelength of 450 nm using a microplate reader to calculate the Hcy content in the serum (11.5, 11.7, 13.07, 21.05, 21.78, 24.39, 25.13, 26.16, 26.51, 34.14, 35.00, 41.42, and 51.26 μM). A sensor array is then used to detect 13 different concentrations of Hcy in the serum samples. Serum samples diluted 100-fold are incubated with four mPH-NPs@Ag+ sensing units for 30 min each. Fluorescence intensity is measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence response data are normalized and analyzed using LDA in SPSS v26.0.

All serum samples are collected from human blood provided by the General Hospital of Northern Theater Command (Shenyang, China), with informed consent obtained from all volunteers in accordance with ethics committee guidelines. All experiments involving human samples and cells are conducted in accordance with relevant laws and institutional guidelines and approved by the Medical Ethics Committee and the Human Clinical Trials Committee of China Medical University.

The chemical structure and composition of the H-CDs and the mPH-NPs are investigated by the characterizations of transmission electron microscope (TEM), Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The morphology of the H-CDs and the mPH-NPs are shown in Fig. 1 a and b. The H-CDs are well dispersed with a spherical structure and an average size of approximately 4.3 ± 0.8 nm (Fig. 1a). The elemental composition and surface functional state of the H-CDs are elucidated through FT-IR and XPS analyses, revealing an abundance of hydroxyl, carboxylic, and nitrogen-containing functional groups on their surface. The FT-IR spectra displayed characteristic absorption peaks for N–H stretching vibrations at approximately 3430 cm−1 (Fig. S9, the blue curve). The vibrational absorption band of C=O at approximately 1718 cm−1, while the stretching vibrations of C–N at 1335 cm−1, and the stretching vibration of C–O at approximately 1200 cm−1 are observed. The XPS spectrum exhibits three strong peaks at 284.6 eV, 400.3 eV, and 531.5 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Figs. S10a–d). The C1s spectrum is decomposed into C-C/C=C (284.4 eV), C-N (285.6 eV), C-O (286.5 eV), C=O (288.7 eV), and COOH (289.5 eV) bonds. The N1s spectrum reveals three nitrogen species: amino N (399.6 eV), pyrrolic N (400.3 eV), and graphitic N (401.6 eV). The O1s spectrum reveals oxygen atoms in the forms of C-O (531.1 eV) and C=O (532.1 eV). The mPH-NPs are spherical nanoparticles. The average particle sizes of mPH-NP-1, mPH-NP-2, mPH-NP-3, and mPH-NP-4 are 89.3 ± 7.2 nm, 95.6 ± 9.9 nm, 105.6 ± 10.2 nm, and 115.2 ± 12.4 nm, respectively (Fig. 1b: mPH-NP-4, Fig. S11: mPH-NP-1, mPH-NP-2, and mPH-NP-3). The H-CDs are confirmed in the mPH-NPs from their FT-IR spectra (Fig. S9 and Fig. S12), and the characteristic stretching vibrations of the H-CDs are also shown in the FT-IR spectra of the mPH-NPs.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum displays two distinct characteristic peaks at 210 nm and 280 nm (Fig. S13, the red curve), attributed to the π−π∗ transitions of aromatic sp2 domains and the n−π∗ transition, respectively (Wu et al., 2017). The H-CDs show characteristic excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior typical of most graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The emission peaks of the H-CDs show a red shift with the increase of the excitation wavelength (λex) from 330 to 370 nm in the PL excitation spectrum (Fig. S13, the blue curve). The maximum emission wavelength (λem) is recorded at 430 nm under the optimal λex of 350 nm. The excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior of GQDs is still unclear, involving various hypotheses, such as multiple surface states and solvent relaxation effects. The possible mechanism of H-CDs involves a combination of factors. The first contribution is multiple surface states. From the FT-IR results, various functional groups are observed on H-CDs. These functional groups create various "surface states" energy levels between the π−π∗ states of C=C. These "surface states" lead to a series of emission traps, dominating emissions at various excitation wavelengths. The relaxation effect of solvent molecules around H-CDs also contributes to the excitation-dependent PL behavior (Khan et al., 2015). Due to the presence of abundant amino groups, H-CDs have low polarity. In polar solvents, H-CDs with low polarity also show excitation-dependent emission, but with narrower peaks and slight redshift due to slow solvent relaxation. When H-CDs are trapped in mPH-NPs, due to the polar environment created by PAsp chains, the PL of H-CDs shows a significant redshift. Encapsulation causes the optimum λex and maximum λem of H-CDs to redshift by 40 nm and 35 nm (mPH-NP-1), 80 nm and 70 nm (mPH-NP-2), 120 nm and 90 nm (mPH-NP-3), 155 nm and 125 nm (mPH-NP-4) (Fig. 1c). The polar PAsp chains enable rapid solvation of H-CDs. This results in complete relaxation of the solvent and lowers the excitation energy of H-CDs before fluorescence decay. The significant redshift PL phenomenon of H-CDs in mPH-NPs can also be attributed to aggregation-induced redshift emission (AIRSE) (Wei et al., 2022). The formation of mPH-NPs induces aggregation of H-CDs, creating a concentrated solution of H-CDs in the core of the micelle. Aggregation can cause π−π∗ conjugation from PL centers of different H-CDs and contribute to a larger conjugated sp2 domain. This reduces the energy gap and extends the electronic system of H-CDs, leading to the PL redshift phenomenon.

A series of PEG-b-PAsp polymers with different PAsp molecular weights are prepared to obtain polyionic composite micelles with different loading amounts and encapsulation efficiencies. The mass feeding ratio of PEG-b-PAsp to the H-CDs is fixed. The mPH-NPs prepared from PEG113-b-PAsp75, PEG113-b-PAsp112, PEG113-b-PAsp153, and PEG113-b-PAsp182 are named as mPH-NP-1, mPH-NP-2, mPH-NP-3, and mPH-NP-4, respectively. The synthetic methods and structural characterization of these copolymers are described in the supporting information (Table S1: Molecular weights and molecular weight distributions; Fig. S1: Synthesis scheme; Figs. S2, S3, S4, S5, and S6: 1H NMR spectra; Fig. S7: GPC spectra; Fig. S8: FT-IR spectra). The zeta potentials of mPH-NP-1, mPH-NP-2, mPH-NP-3, and mPH-NP-4 are −19.2 mV, −21.3 mV, −22.9 mV, and −25.4 mV, respectively (Fig. 1d). The increased zeta potentials of the mPH-NPs indicate their enhanced stability with the increase of the molecular weights of PAsp. The loading amounts and encapsulation efficiency of the mPH-NPs are also raised with the increase of the molecular weights of PAsp (Table S2). The loading contents and encapsulation efficiencies of the mPH-NPs are determined by the organic solvent extraction method based on the standard curve of the H-CDs (Fig. S14). A detailed description of this method is provided in the Supporting Information. It is well understood that PAsp with higher molecular weights means that they have more negative charges and can adsorb more positive H-CDs. The encapsulation efficiency of the mPH-NP-4 can achieve 53.61%, and the loading efficiency can achieve 51.67%.

In the integrated sensor array system, the mPH-NPs create a high-concentration solution of H-CDs and form an ionic cage for the sensing process. Meanwhile, mPH-NPs significantly enhance the non-covalent electrostatic attraction among PAsp chains, H-CDs, and Ag+. The electrostatic cross-linking core structure of mPH-NPs, characterized by high porosity and numerous binding sites, effectively traps thiols. The ionic sensing cage within mPH-NPs reduces the interaction distance between the H-CDs, Ag+, and analyte, thereby enhancing the sensing efficiency. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) confirm the sensing process occurs within the core of the micelles. TEM images of mPH-NPs, mPH-NPs@Ag+, and mPH-NPs@Ag+ after adding glutathione (GSH) show an unchanged nanoscopic structure, maintaining the spherical micelle appearance (Fig. 2 a). SEM and elemental mapping images display similar results (Fig. 2b). The uniform distribution of C, N, O, Ag, and S elements within the core of the micelles indicates that the integrated sensing detection process occurs primarily within the core, enhancing detection stability. The UV–vis spectroscopy analysis further confirmed the absorbance changes in mPH-NPs after the sequential addition of Ag⁺ and GSH (Fig. S19). Initially, the mPH-NPs exhibit a pronounced absorption peak at approximately 250 nm (blue line). Compared to H-CDs, the absorption peak in mPH-NPs is enhanced and red-shifted, reflecting changes in the emission spectra under optimal excitation wavelengths. Upon successive additions of Ag⁺ (green line) and GSH (orange line), the absorption peak's position remained largely unchanged, indicating that the mPH-NPs structure remains stable and does not undergo significant disassembly. The absorbance initially decreases and then increases, corresponding to the variation in binding interactions between mPH-NPs and Ag⁺ before and after the addition of GSH.

The sensor array is constructed by combining mPH-NP-1@Ag+, mPH-NP-2@Ag+, mPH-NP-3@Ag+, and mPH-NP-4@Ag+. Seven thiols (MA-SH, ME-SH, MS-SH, CH-SH, GSH, Hcy, and Cys) are chosen for analytical assessment (Table S5). Initially, eight representative thiol concentrations within the range of 0.5–1000 μM are established to assess their impact on the sensor array. Principal component analysis (PCA) is employed for the examination and illustration of the luminescence response patterns of the training matrix (4 sensors × 7 thiols × 5 replicates). The analysis shows that with the increase in thiol concentration, the distribution of data points for different types of thiols also changes correspondingly (Fig. 3 a). This signifies an augmented capacity of the sensing system for discerning thiols within elevated concentration spectra. In contrast, the impact of seven thiols on the fluorescence intensity of four sensing units (mPH-NPs) is negligible, demonstrating that metal ions play a primary role in the fluorescence response (Fig. S20). Furthermore, the discernment capacity of the sensor array is appraised via the mean Euclidean distance (ED) among the dataset (Fig. 3b). A larger average ED value indicates greater data dispersion, signifying better discriminative capability of the sensor system. The increase in average ED value with thiol concentration indicates a more accurate differentiation effect of the sensor array system for thiols. Subsequently, by analyzing the response data for thiols at two low concentrations (0.5 μM and 1 μM), the minimum concentration for qualitative identification of thiols using the sensor is further investigated (Figs. 3c, S21a and Tables S8, S10, S24, and S25). At both concentrations, data points for each analyte can be differentiated. However, the data points from five repeated measurements of the same analyte form a tighter cluster at 0.5 μM, indicating that this concentration approaches the detection limit for the sensor system's detection capabilities. The heatmap is simultaneously generated in response to different thiols, serving as a "fingerprint" spectrum, revealing the feasibility of the sensor array in distinguishing the seven thiols at concentrations of 0.5 μM and 1 μM (Fig. 3d and Figs. S21b, S21e-f). Furthermore, the LDA analysis results for the two low concentrations (0.5 μM and 1 μM) of thiols are consistent with the aforementioned results (Figs. S21c–d and Tables S9 and S11). The aforementioned results indicate that the sensing system can achieve a lower limit for thiol identification and differentiation at 0.5 μM.

The accuracy and practicality of the sensor array system are assessed via a double-blind experiment (Fig. 4 a and Tables S12 and S26). The sensing mechanism examines three unknown samples (UN-1, UN-2, and UN-3) from the training collection and evaluates the standardized fluorescence (FL) responses employing PCA grounded in the initial training matrix. Based on the principle of the shortest Mahalanobis distance, all blind samples are accurately categorized into their respective types (UN-1 = GSH, UN-2=Hcy, and UN-3=Cys). The results demonstrate that the sensor array exhibits good performance in identifying and differentiating thiols. The selectivity and specificity of the sensor array are evaluated through interference experiments (Fig. 4b and Tables S13 and S27). To verify the anti-interference ability of the sensor array, several potential interfering compounds (all at a concentration of 100 μM) are introduced into the sensing system for interference experiments. Even at a concentration that is 100 times that of the thiol concentration (1 μM), the sensing unit shows no significant fluorescence response to various proteins, metal ions, amino acids, urea, and biomolecular compounds. This indicates that high-concentration interfering substances do not affect the sensing system's recognition of thiols and that they can be used for detection in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Qualitative analysis of thiols in serum
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility and practicality of the mPH-NPs@Ag+ sensing unit in real samples, this sensor array system is used to detect and analyze thiols in serum environments. The sensing system accurately identifies seven thiols in serum samples (Fig. S22a and Tables S14 and S28).

Accurate detection of GSH in serum environments helps predict the risk of various cancer diseases, while Hcy, as the only risk factor for cardiovascular diseases, also receives much attention (Zhu et al., 2022). However, identifying thiol mixtures remains challenging due to their similar structures and differences in concentration and mole ratios of analytes, which may alter response patterns. Therefore, GSH, Hcy, and Cys binary and ternary mixtures are selected as research models in serum environment samples to further investigate the identification ability of the sensor array for thiol mixtures. To determine the multiple discrimination ability of the sensor array, a series of binary and ternary mixtures with different combinations and mole ratios are prepared and detected (Figs. S22b-e and Tables S15–S18, S29–S32). Binary thiol mixtures (GSH: Hcy = 100%: 0%, 20%: 80%, 30%: 70%, 40%: 60%, 50%: 50%, 60%: 40%, 70%: 30%, 80%: 20% and 0: 100%; GSH: Cys = 100%: 0%, 20%: 80%, 30%: 70%, 40%: 60%, 50%: 50%, 60%: 40%, 70%: 30%, 80%: 20% and 0: 100%; Hcy: Cys = 100%: 0%, 20%: 80%, 30%: 70%, 40%: 60%, 50%: 50%, 60%: 40%, 70%: 30%, 80%: 20% and 0: 100%; total concentration of 10 μM) and ternary thiol mixtures (GSH: Hcy: Cys = 10%: 10%: 80%, 20%: 20%: 60%, 30%: 30%: 40%, 40%: 40%: 20%, 60%: 20%: 20%, 100% GSH, 100% Hcy, 100% Cys; total concentration of 10 μM) are selected as target analytes. All thiols in different mole ratios of mixtures cluster well into different groups without any overlap, and data are analyzed by heat maps (Figs. S22f–j), achieving 100% accuracy for mutual separation and differentiation, laying a foundation for identifying and differentiating complex real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative analysis of thiols in serum
ce:section.ce:para: The determination of the concentrations of GSH, Hcy, and Cys in serum not only supports the early diagnosis of various types of cancer but also relates to the staging, treatment, and prognosis of cardiovascular diseases (Lei et al., 2016). The typical score chart for the analysis of GSH, Hcy, and Cys under serum conditions shows a fluorescence response mode that is correlated with their concentrations. Taking GSH as an example (Fig. 5 a and Tables S19 and S33), the solid data points of the GSH training set samples at different concentrations show a progressively separated data point set with obvious separation. The concentration-dependent distribution of the analyte sample is based on the fluorescence progressive changes induced by the interaction of the mPH-NPs@Ag+ with GSH. The training set samples for Hcy and Cys also show similar dependency patterns (Figs. 5b and c and Tables S20, S21, S34, and S35). Each sample within the prognosticated set (represented by hollow data points) falls within the appropriate concentration spectrum. The obtained training set sample data is analyzed by LDA to establish the concentration standard curve of the ED. Within the 1–300 μM concentration span, the EDs for the GSH, Hcy, and Cys training samples exhibit a robust linear correlation with their respective concentrations (Figs. 5d–f). The ED of the predicted set samples are substituted into the ED concentration standard curve calibration model to obtain the concentrations of the predicted set samples. The forecasted concentrations (PC) for GSH samples in the predicted set (100 μM and 180 μM) register at 102.634 μM and 184.219 μM, respectively. The prediction error rate (PER) signifies that the model boasts substantial predictive precision (> 90%).

Regression analysis of fluorescence response data for GSH, Hcy, and Cys using the SVM achieves accurate concentration quantification in a single-thiol system. The gathered eight data arrays are segmented into two sections, with six sets designated for calibration and modeling, while the remaining two are utilized for cross-validation. The constructed model distinctly elucidates data variation patterns, thereby facilitating precise determination of the sample concentrations via the data model. The model demonstrates satisfactory calibration performance, and visual comparison of the predicted versus actual concentrations of GSH, Hcy, and Cys in the training set shows minimal error. The concentrations of GSH, Hcy, and Cys in the testing set samples are very close to the actual concentrations in the training set (GSH: 101.466 μM/100 μM, 182.733 μM/180 μM; Hcy: 102.472 μM/100 μM, 179.814 μM/180 μM; Cys: 100.402 μM/100 μM, 180.692 μM/180 μM). The root mean square error (RMSE) for calibration (C), cross-validation (CV), and prediction (P) shows that the correlation coefficients for standard samples with both internal and external cross-validation exceed 90%, thus demonstrating the high reliability of the predictive model (Figs. 5g–i). The limits of detection (LOD) are 0.04 μM for GSH, 0.11 μM for Hcy, and 0.06 μM for Cys, while the limits of quantification (LOQ) (3.3-fold LOD) are 0.132 μM for GSH, 0.363 μM for Hcy, and 0.198 μM for Cys respectively. The sensor array exhibits excellent quantitative analysis capability for GSH, Hcy, and Cys in single-thiol systems within serum environments.

Quantitative analysis of multi-component mixtures in complex sample environments is another significant challenge. Employing a sensor array, the study processes a series of ternary mixtures of GSH, Hcy, and Cys in serum to achieve precise quantification. To accurately quantify multicomponent mixtures, a sensor array was used to analyze a series of ternary combinations of GSH, Hcy, and Cys in serum. The fluorescence responses of GSH, Hcy, and Cys in the ternary mixtures are analyzed using a SVM to determine the specific concentrations of each component in the mixtures (Figs. S23a–c). Concentration ranges for GSH, Hcy, and Cys are set at 0–200 μM, 0–120 μM, and 0–160 μM, respectively. Predicted concentrations for GSH, Hcy, and Cys closely align with actual concentrations from the validation dataset (GSH: 46.2 μM/45 μM and 90.4 μM/90 μM; Hcy: 30.2 μM/30 μM and 60.5 μM/60 μM; Cys: 41.6 μM/40 μM and 80.2 μM/80 μM). Correlation coefficients exceed 90% for all analytes. These results confirm the sensor array's capability for quantitative detection of GSH, Hcy, and Cys within ternary mixtures in serum environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of thiols in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicality of the mPH-NP@Ag+ sensor array in biomedical applications, LDA is used to qualitatively and quantitatively analyze various cell types based on different thiol concentrations in the samples. Glutathione (GSH) is reported as a crucial biomarker for cancer, its concentration is higher in cancer cells than in normal cells (Zhu et al., 2022). Therefore, monitoring the GSH content in cell samples becomes an effective method for cancer diagnosis. The GSH concentrations in normal liver cells (L02), normal lung tissue cells (BEAS-2B), lung cancer cells (A549), cervical cancer cells (HeLa), and liver cancer cells (HepG2) are measured to verify the potential application of the sensor array. After processing with LDA, cells are accurately classified (Fig. 6 a and Tables S22 and S36). The data is inputted into the ED concentration standard calibration model, obtaining the concentrations for the cell prediction set samples, with GSH content in normal cells (L02, BEAS-2B) below 10 μM, predicted at 5.2 μM and 8.7 μM respectively. The GSH content in cancer cells (A549, HeLa, and HepG2) exceeds 10 μM, with predicted values of 13.5 μM, 16.7 μM, and 18.5 μM respectively (Fig. 6b). All the aforementioned prediction results are within the quantitative analysis range of the GSH assay kit, indicating that this model is applicable to practical detection applications (Fig. S24 and Table S6). A significant difference in GSH concentrations leads to the clustering of cancer cells (A549, HeLa, and HepG2) on the right side of the two-dimensional canonical score plot, while normal cell clusters (L02, BEAS-2B) are located on the left. The violin plot reveals significant differences in Discriminant Factor 1 between normal and cancer cells (Fig. 6c). To further assess disease identification performance, LDA is utilized to plot the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve, with the ordinate representing sensitivity (true positive rate, TPR) and the abscissa representing 1-specificity (false positive rate, FPR). The area under the ROC curve (AUC) reflects the performance of the diagnostic test, with an AUC closer to 1 indicating a better diagnostic outcome (Fig. 6d). In an ideal diagnosis, a TPR of 1 and an FPR of 0 demonstrate the potential of the sensor array for high-precision differentiation between cancer and healthy states.

Elevated Hcy levels are a risk factor for peripheral vascular, cerebrovascular, and coronary artery diseases. In addition to affecting lipid metabolism, high Hcy can induce cardiovascular diseases through various mechanisms. For example, elevated Hcy levels can impair endothelial function, increase oxidative stress, and enhance inflammatory responses, thereby accelerating the progression of atherosclerosis and increasing the risk of thrombosis (Zhou et al., 2023). Therefore, managing Hcy levels is crucial for the prevention and treatment of cardiovascular diseases. It has been reported that the concentration of Hcy in the blood of healthy individuals typically ranges from 5 to 15 μM. For every 5 μM/L increase in Hcy levels, the pooled relative risk (RR) for coronary heart disease mortality is 1.52, for cardiovascular disease mortality is 1.32, and for all-cause mortality is 1.27 (Cui et al., 2022). This study analyzed 13 serum samples with varying Hcy concentrations. These included three samples from healthy volunteers and ten from cardiovascular disease (CVD) patients. A 2D canonical score plot is generated using the LDA model (Fig. 6e and Tables S23 and S37). Notably, the three samples with Hcy concentrations below 15 μM are clustered on the left side of the plot, while the ten samples with concentrations exceeding 15 μM are positioned on the right, with no overlap between the groups. The classification accuracy reaches 100%, demonstrating the potential of the proposed sensor array for identifying CVD. The Hcy concentrations are predicted using an ED concentration standard calibration model. For healthy volunteers, the predicted values are 11.42 μM, 11.63 μM, and 13.08 μM, all below the 15 μM threshold. For CVD patients, the predicted Hcy concentrations are 21.92 μM, 22.15 μM, 24.95 μM, 25.12 μM, 26.1 μM, 26.5 μM, 33.76 μM, 34.68 μM, 41.44 μM, and 50.95 μM (Figs. 6f and S25). These results are consistent with the ELISA detection range for healthy volunteers and CVD patients, indicating that the model is suitable for clinical testing applications (Table S7). Moreover, the violin plot illustrates a significant difference in the scores of discriminant factor 1 between healthy individuals and CVD patients (Fig. 6g). Based on the ROC curve (AUC = 1, TPR = 1, FPR = 0), the sensor array exhibits excellent diagnostic performance for cardiovascular disease (Fig. 6h). Overall, the mPH-NPs@Ag+ sensor array demonstrates significant potential as a diagnostic tool for clinical applications, particularly in the identification and diagnosis of cardiovascular disease.

In conclusion, this study develops an All-in-one sensor array based on polyionic complex micelles (mPH-NPs), capable of successfully distinguishing seven types of thiol analytes (MA-SH, ME-SH, MS-SH, CH-SH, GSH, Hcy, and Cys) within a broad concentration range of 0.5–1000 μM. The integrated sensing strategy enables the detection process to occur within the core of the micelles, where the limited space and ionic environment inside the micelles shorten the interaction distance between the H-CDs, Ag+, and analyte, effectively enhancing the sensitivity of the sensor array and lowering the detection limit of the analytes. Notably, the application of machine learning algorithms (PCA, LDA, and SVM) enables qualitative and quantitative analysis of both single and multi-component thiol systems, with an accuracy rate exceeding 90%. Moreover, this strategy demonstrates potential applications in the identification of thiol-related diseases, such as cancer and cardiovascular diseases. We believe these research outcomes will facilitate the development of high-sensitivity sensors for biological thiol biomarkers, providing a convenient tool for the efficient diagnosis of thiol-related diseases.

Hongyang Niu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Haichun Li: Investigation. Yi Wang: Methodology, Conceptualization. Bingtao Hu: Methodology, Conceptualization. Xue Liu: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51873085), Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province-Outstanding Youth Foundation (2022-YQ-14), and China Scholarship Council (CSC Scholarship No. 202006800009).
